meta.title: Readlock Final Test
meta.description: Every rule a finished draft must pass. If a rule fails, open the guide at the section named in the ref attribute. This file only checks.

block.name: Do only what was asked

rule.ref: guide: Do only what was asked blockquote

block.rule: Zero extra packages, swipes, sentences, or explanations beyond what the prompt requested.

rule.ref: guide: Do only what was asked blockquote

block.rule: When in doubt between shorter and longer, the draft is shorter.

block.name: Identity
block.rule: Voice: Veritasium or Kurzgesagt, not textbook, TED talk, LinkedIn post, friend texting, salesman, or forcing teacher.
No authority posture, no performed insight, no urgency, no fear, no pressure.
The package reads as a calm essayist who respects the reader.

block.name: Relationship with the Reader

rule.ref: guide: Section 3, Never tell them what they think

block.rule: Zero narration of the reader's inner life ("You think...", "You feel...", "You just processed...").

rule.ref: guide: Section 3, Never tell them what they think

block.rule: Zero identity assignments ("You have an incredible superpower", "You are not just X, you are Y").
Zero condescending explanations. Zero congratulating the reader for understanding something simple.
"We" appears ONLY in humanity-wide observations ("we figure out most objects without reading the manual"). Zero uses of "you" to address the reader anywhere in course text.
Reader-immersion scenes (Variant A) use a gerund or imperative ("Imagine standing..."), never "we" and never "you." Zero "we are standing in a kitchen" or similar fake-intimacy "we."
Invitations only ("Imagine standing at..."), never assertions about what the reader did or is.

When the reader is likely forming a guess, meet them there with phrases like "This seems counterintuitive at first" or "At first glance, the answer seems obvious" or "By now, the question of X is probably forming". Acknowledge the shared thinking without assigning thoughts.

rule.ref: guide: Section 3, Never assume who they are

block.rule: Zero assumptions about job, age, education, or context.

group.name: Flow integrity
group.rule: The package reads as one unbroken line of thought. No swipe out of order.
The reader does not arrive at the insight before Phase 4.

group.name: Phase 1, Intro
group.rule: Exactly 1 intro swipe (or zero, if the package uses the skip-intro variant), 2 to 4 sentences when @text. The swipe type can be @text (default), @quote, @true_false, OR a misconception-first @text. See "Other ways to start a package" in the guide.

Default intro opens with a broad human hook and does not name the topic. Exception: a misconception-first intro CAN name the everyday term for the topic, with the wrong part wrapped in <c:r>...</c:r>.
Opener style rotates across packages. Same type never repeated consecutively.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, How to make the reader curious

group.rule: Every line of the intro puts a picture in the reader's head. Visualisation is the test, not just word count.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, How to make the reader curious

group.rule: Use picture-able nouns ("coffee cup", "stuck door", "light switch"), not invisible abstract nouns ("system", "process", "concept", "context", "framework", "approach").

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, How to make the reader curious

group.rule: Use verbs the reader can watch happening ("whisper", "lean", "tug", "slip", "crack open", "fumble"), not flat explainer verbs ("communicate", "represent", "involve", "is about").

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, How to make the reader curious

group.rule: End the intro on something unfinished. The last sentence leaves a question in the reader's mind, a "but" that flips what came before, or a small contradiction. A flat ending kills curiosity.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, How to make the reader curious

group.rule: Zero intros that answer their own setup. If the intro already explains the idea, the next swipe has nothing to do.

group.name: Intro variants (when used)

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Other ways to start a package

group.rule: Quote intro: 1 @quote swipe with a real, verifiable, attributed quote. Zero invented quotes. Once per segment maximum.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Other ways to start a package

group.rule: True/false intro: 1 @true_false swipe. Statement is visual, does not name the topic, only "true" and "false" are options. Once per segment maximum. When used, the package has zero @true_false swipes in Phase 5 (the intro replaces it).

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Other ways to start a package

group.rule: Misconception-first intro: 1 @text swipe stating a wrong belief, with the wrong part wrapped in <c:r>...</c:r>. Phase 2 must contradict the misconception. Once per segment maximum.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Other ways to start a package

group.rule: Skip-intro: a package can begin directly at Phase 2 if the first story is strong enough. Once per segment maximum.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Other ways to start a package

group.rule: No two intro variants in the same package. Pick one per package, never two.

group.name: Phase 2, Stories / Narratives
group.rule: 2 to 3 consecutive @text swipes, each 5 to 7 sentences. Each sentence targets ~14 words (can overflow to 17-18).

Package follows one of 7 variants (A-G) or a mix. Swipe structure matches the variant. Variants are starting points, not rigid templates, and can be mixed. The variants: A = reader-immersion scenario (gerund or imperative, never "you"/"we"); B = single person narrative; C = two (or more) people contrast; D = diverse multiple stories across contexts; E = scientific walkthrough (observation → mechanism → result); F = historical arc across eras; G = thought experiment ("Suppose...").
Stories create a question, not a conclusion.
"Suppose..." and "Imagine..." prefixes are optional. A scene can start directly without them.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 2, Stories can include a line of dialogue

group.rule: Variants A, B, C, D, and F can include a short line of dialogue when it brings the scene closer to the reader. Variants E and G never include dialogue. One line per swipe, two at most. No back-and-forth scripts.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 2, Stories can include a line of dialogue

group.rule: Dialogue is written as people actually talk, plain words, no theatre voice. The narrator never comments on the spoken line or explains what it means.

group.name: Phase 3, Questions
group.rule: 1 to 2 swipes using @question or @true_false, rarely 3. No @estimate here.
Question tone is curious, not testing. Reader feels safe.
Every question is answerable from what came before.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 3, Sometimes Phase 3 can be skipped completely

group.rule: Phase 3 can be skipped entirely when the story phase already brings the reader to the realisation. When skipped, the package goes from Phase 2 directly to Phase 4. Use this rarely, at most once per segment.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 3, Vary question openers across packages

group.rule: Question openers vary across packages. Zero segments where every @question starts with "What...". Rotate through "Why...", "How...", "Where...", or sentence-first openers. No two consecutive packages use the same interrogative. If a segment has 3 or more packages with questions, at least 2 different openers must appear.

group.name: Consequences
group.rule: 1 sentence default, 2 occasional, 3 rare. Should almost never exceed 20 words total.
Zero verdict language ("Wrong.", "Incorrect.", "This would not work.").
Wrong-but-intuitive: brief acknowledgment then correction. Sometimes just the correction. Vary the approach.
Wrong-but-not-intuitive: plain correction, no invented reasoning.
Correct answers: brief confirmation plus reasoning. No "Exactly!" or "Great job!".
Zero sugarcoating. Wrong is wrong, stated honestly.
Nuance beyond 2 sentences belongs in explanation (explanation:), not consequence.

group.name: Hints
group.rule: Exactly one sentence, at most 14 words (can overflow to 17-18 when needed).
Points at the topic of the question, not at the answer.
Also useful as context the reader carries forward.
Not a rephrasing of the question.

group.name: Explanations
group.rule: 1 to 2 sentences. Not a paragraph. Each sentence targets ~14 words.

group.name: Phase 4, Topic Reveal
group.rule: 1 to 2 @text swipes, 5 to 7 sentences each.
Names the pattern. Makes the connection explicit. Feels like a click, not a lecture.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Do not mention the source book or its author

group.rule: Zero mentions of source book or author in course text. Only in @course metadata.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Vary how you name the topic

group.rule: Topic naming varies across packages. Zero repetitive "Researchers call/describe this as..." formula.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Do not lean on the same attribution word twice

group.rule: Reveal sentence does not lean on the same attribution word that already appeared in Phase 2. If "researchers," "scientists," "study," or "experiment" was used in the stories, the reveal opens with a different construction.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Never break the immersion with classroom voice

group.rule: Zero meta-narration in the reveal. Banned openers: "We have seen...", "We just saw...", "As we have just learned...", "Now it is time to...", "Now let us...", "Now we will see...", "Let us look at...", "Let us name...", "Let us turn to...", "What all three stories have in common is...". Banned verbs as the main verb of the reveal sentence with narrator-and-reader as subject: see, look at, examine, turn to.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Never break the immersion with classroom voice

group.rule: The reveal sentence stays inside the same scene language as Phase 2. The topic name lands inside a sentence that is still doing the work of the scene (mentions the kitchen, the museum, the watch, etc.), not after a "now let me explain" lead-in.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Never break the immersion with classroom voice

group.rule: Read-aloud test: the reveal sentence flows directly from the last Phase 2 sentence with no tonal shift. If reading the two sentences in a row triggers an "and now..." classroom shift, the reveal failed.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, The reveal sentence must connect the concept to the main idea of the whole course

group.rule: Zero label-only reveal sentences. "This effect is called X" and "This has a name: Y" fail. They are too general and could appear in any course.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, The reveal sentence must connect the concept to the main idea of the whole course

group.rule: The sentence with the green term connects the concept to the main idea of the whole course. Examples of a course main idea: for Emotional Design, "three layers of how design reaches inside the person"; for habits, "the four parts of the habit loop"; for cognitive biases, "the shortcuts the mind reaches for".

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, The reveal sentence must connect the concept to the main idea of the whole course

group.rule: Swap test: replace the green term with any concept from a different course. If the sentence still fits, it was too general and must be rewritten.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, The reveal sentence must connect the concept to the main idea of the whole course

group.rule: Main-idea test: read the reveal sentence alone. A reader can tell what the whole course is about from this one sentence. If not, the sentence is a label and must be rewritten.

group.name: Color highlights

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Color highlights, rule 1

group.rule: Green marks the topic only. Appears once in Phase 4. May repeat once if same term. Only inside @text.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Color highlights, rule 2

group.rule: Red marks only a wrong thing. Never on the topic, never on anything correct. Only inside @text.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Color highlights, rule 3

group.rule: 1 to 2 highlights per package total across both colors.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Color highlights, rule 4

group.rule: At most 1 highlight per @text swipe. No two highlights in the same swipe.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Color highlights, rule 5

group.rule: Highlights wrap the exact term, not a whole sentence.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 4, Color highlights, rule 6

group.rule: Zero highlights on generic words ("decision", "number", "thing").
Zero color markup inside @question, @true_false, @estimate, @pause, @reflect, @quote, or any property field.

group.name: Optional @pause
group.rule: Zero or one per package. Single sentence. Goes after Phase 3 (questions), before Phase 4 (reveal).
Small interesting fact or reframing. Not a quiz, recap, or motivational line.
Zero "Did you know...", "Fun fact:", "Pro tip:".
Must not name or hint at the topic. The reader has not reached Phase 4 yet.

group.name: Optional @estimate
group.rule: Zero or one per package. After Phase 4. Only when topic has a numerical element.

group.name: Phase 5, True/False
group.rule: 1 to 2 @true_false swipes. Statement on one line.
Statement is semi-obvious after a moment of thought. Not dumb, not a trap.
The two answer options are always literally "True" and "False". Never descriptive, never paraphrased. The text after true: and false: is the consequence, not the option label.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Writing @question consequences and hints

group.rule: Consequences and hints follow the same rules as @question.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 5, Phase 5 is skipped if the package opens with a true/false intro

group.rule: If the package opened with a true/false intro, Phase 5 is empty for that package. The package never has two @true_false swipes when one of them is the intro.

group.name: Phase 6, Reflect (optional)
group.rule: 0 or 1 @reflect swipe. Optional. Skip when the package arc feels complete without it. Phase 6 contains @reflect only, no @text.
When present: 2 or 3 points, each 14 to 16 words.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 6, Each reflect point needs one concrete example

group.rule: Each point contains one concrete example: a situation, an object, a moment, or a type of person.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 6, Reflect points should not be cliche

group.rule: Zero platitudes, zero fridge-magnet wisdom.
Reflect points use no "you." Phrase as a noun phrase the reader can match to their own life ("A kitchen tool kept for its looks alone:..."), not a direct address.

group.name: Package outro
group.rule: Every package ends with a @text outro as its LAST swipe. This is mandatory for all packages.
2 to 3 sentences. Never longer. Each sentence targets ~14 words.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Package outro, For non-last packages

group.rule: Non-last packages: outro closes the thought and weaves in a transition to the next package's topic.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Package outro, Vary transition phrasing

group.rule: Transition phrasing varies across packages. Zero repetitive "The next package asks/looks at/examines..." formula.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Package outro, For the last package of the course

group.rule: Last package: names the thread of the course without listing packages. Ends on a question or alternative angle, not a resolution.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Package outro, For the last package of the course

group.rule: Zero recap language ("In conclusion", "To summarize", "We covered", "And that changes everything").

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Package outro, Vary the opener, the closer, and the sentence count

group.rule: The structure is not copied from the guide's examples. Every outro sounds different.

group.name: Consecutive @text swipes

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Length reference, no more than 3 @text swipes in a row

group.rule: No more than 3 @text in a row anywhere, not counting Phase 1 intro.

group.name: Package totals
group.rule: Total swipes per package: 7 to 11.

Default phase order: 1 → 2 → 3 → (optional pause) → 4 → (optional estimate) → 5 → (optional reflect) → outro. Phases that can be skipped: 1 (skip-intro variant), 3 (when stories already land the realisation), 5 (when intro was @true_false), 6 (always optional).

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Length reference, First package of the first segment is always free

group.rule: First package of first segment is marked Free.

group.name: Structural variation across the segment

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Other ways to start a package

group.rule: Intro variants (skip-intro, quote, true/false, misconception-first) are available alongside the default @text intro. Each variant is capped at once per segment when used. Zero is also fine.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Phase 3, Vary question openers across packages

group.rule: Question openers are varied across packages: no two consecutive packages share the same interrogative, and a 3+ question segment uses at least 2 different openers.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Package outro, Vary transition phrasing

group.rule: Outro transitions vary across packages. Zero repetitive "The next package asks/looks at/examines..." formula. Vary the opener, the closer, and the sentence count across packages.

group.name: Course totals

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Length reference, Course level

group.rule: Segments per course: 2 to 3 maximum.

rule.ref: guide: Section 5, Length reference, Course level

group.rule: Packages per segment: 3 to 5 maximum.

group.name: Claims and evidence
group.rule: Every claim anchored in history, evolution, or a scenario. Zero isolated facts.
Every number has a visual anchor the reader can picture.
Put the picture first or wrap it around the number. Never lead with the raw number. Correct: "70,000 years ago, long before any city existed". Wrong: "In 70,000 BCE, a population appeared."
Visual anchors are universal. Zero obviously local references ("Super Bowl", "when your grandparents were in school").

Every quantity 10 and above is in digits, always. The boundary is inclusive: 10 itself is digits, not "ten". Correct: "10 years", "12 seconds", "70,000 years ago". Wrong: "ten years", "twelve seconds", "seventy thousand years ago".

Small numbers that read naturally as words ("one", "two", "three") can stay as words in body text. The test: is the reader supposed to picture a specific amount? If yes, use digits. If the number is just a count that flows with the sentence, words are fine.

Number format is US English. Comma for thousands ("1,200", "70,000", "299,792 kilometers per second"). Period for decimals ("3.14"). Zero spaces as separators, zero periods for thousands. Wrong: "1.200", "70 000", "3,14".
Percentages use the % symbol. Write "1%" not "1 percent". Use the word "percent" only when the % symbol would look odd in the sentence.
Years are plain digits with no comma: "1969", "1789", "170". Zero thousands separator in years. Decades as "the 1920s", never "the nineteen-twenties".
Times of day in digits, with am/pm ("10am", "3pm") or 24-hour ("10:00", "15:30"). Never "ten", "three o'clock", or "half past two".
Zero subjective claims presented as universal truth.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Claims and evidence, Every abstract idea needs an anchor from everyday life

group.rule: Every abstract concept in the package has ONE everyday anchor next to it (an object, scene, or moment from ordinary life that the reader has actually lived). Examples: snowflake-and-avalanche for compound effects, parking meter for anchoring bias, kitchen drawer for the behavioral level.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Claims and evidence, Every abstract idea needs an anchor from everyday life

group.rule: The anchor must be something the reader has actually lived, not a poetic image or metaphor for sounding clever.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Claims and evidence, Every abstract idea needs an anchor from everyday life

group.rule: One anchor per concept, never two. If two anchors appear for the same concept in one package, the first was too weak — replace it, do not stack.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Claims and evidence, Every abstract idea needs an anchor from everyday life

group.rule: Retention test: after one read, can the concept be recalled by recalling the anchor? If no, the anchor failed.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Claims and evidence, Every abstract idea needs an anchor from everyday life

group.rule: Zero abstract dictionary-entry sentences ("X describes how...", "Y is when..."). Every concept arrives wrapped around a concrete picture from daily life.

group.name: Word choice
group.rule: Plain words for plain concepts. Zero inflated substitutions.
Zero redundant words. "In order to" is "to". "Make a decision" is "decide".

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, No things or stuff

group.rule: Zero "things" or "stuff". Zero placeholder nouns ("factors", "aspects", "elements", "matters").

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, No like as example-intro

group.rule: Zero "like" as example-introducer. Use "such as", a colon, or a separate sentence.
Register between CEFR B1 and B2, closer to B2. A B1 reader should understand every sentence without a dictionary.
Zero obscure idioms. Well-known B1 idioms allowed only when the plain version would be clunkier.
Zero rare phrasal verbs unless plain version would genuinely weaken the sentence.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Idioms and phrasal verbs, combined cap

group.rule: One idiom OR one rare phrasal verb per package, maximum. Not one of each. Zero is fine.
Zero rare or made-up hyphenated words ("half-second", "split-second", "mid-thought", "near-miss") unless the plain word would lose meaning. Default to the plain word.

group.name: Sentence construction

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, One idea per line

group.rule: Every @text line is one thought.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, One sentence per line

group.rule: One sentence per line default. Up to two when they land as one beat. Three: never.
Every line targets ~16 words total. Single sentence targets ~14 (can go to 17-18 occasionally). When a line holds two sentences, they together still fit in ~16 words.
Zero fragments anywhere, including inside two-sentence beats. Both halves of a two-sentence beat must be full sentences. "Not accomplishments." is banned.
Merge choppy sentence pairs that form one thought. "Goals end. Identities persist." becomes "Goals end, but identities persist."
Every metaphor is clear, relatable, B1-readable. Metaphors that exist to sound clever are removed.
Zero tangled metaphors. No stacking. One per sentence maximum.
If the literal version is the same length as the metaphor, use the literal version.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, Every list of 3 or more items needs 'or' or 'and' before the last item

group.rule: Every list of 3+ items uses "or" or "and" before the last item. Zero drumrolls. Two-item lists also use a connector.

group.name: Craft techniques
group.rule: Plain reframes only when genuinely needed, not reflexively.
Draft reads naturally aloud. Zero stumbles, zero fragments.
Zero em dashes (—) anywhere in any swipe or property field. Use a comma, period, colon, or parentheses instead.
Zero ascending lists of three, dramatic pauses, rhetorical Q-then-A, callback lines, "let that sink in."

block.name: Banned Words and Phrases
block.rule: Zero banned verbs: leverage, utilize, delve, foster, elevate, empower, resonate, underscore, navigate (metaphorical), unlock (metaphorical).
Zero banned nouns/adjectives: synergy, paradigm, holistic, pivotal, multifaceted, game-changer, tapestry, realm, landscape (metaphorical), arguably.
Zero filler phrases: "at the end of the day", "it goes without saying", "in today's world", "it is worth noting that", "it turns out that", "when it comes to", "in order to".
Zero pseudo-profound: "at its core", "the power of", "serves as a testament to".

group.name: Contrast and reversal

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero correctio in any form: the "not X, it's Y" move (Latin: correctio; Greek: epanorthosis). Banned as one sentence ("It's not X, it's Y."), as two sentences ("It is not X. It is Y."), softened ("It's not just X, it's Y." / "Not so much X as Y."), and as definition-then-reframe. If Y is the real claim, write only Y.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero mirror sentences ("The question is not whether X, the question is Y.").

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero definition-then-reframe ("X is defined as Y. But what it really means is Z.").

group.name: Inflation and enumeration

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero triple-adjective lists.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero triple-noun drumrolls. If 3 nouns needed, "or"/"and" before the last.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero list-of-three with ascending intensity.

group.name: Dramatic framing

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero colon-punchlines.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero false profundity closers ("And that changes everything").

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero emotional imperatives ("Let that sink in", "Read that again").

group.name: Rhetorical manipulation

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero rhetorical questions immediately answered.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero "imagine if" escalation chains.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero bookend callbacks.

group.name: Weak references

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero sentences starting with "This." as vague reference.

rule.ref: guide: Section 4, AI sentence structures to avoid

group.rule: Zero paragraphs ending with a question the next paragraph immediately answers.

group.name: Inflated assertions

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Tone pitfalls to avoid

group.rule: Zero inflated assertions: "The math is real.", "That number is not a metaphor.", "The science is settled.", "The data is clear.", "The evidence speaks for itself."

group.name: Choppy sentence pairs
group.rule: Zero choppy sentence pairs that should be one sentence. "Goals end. Identities persist." must be written as "Goals end, but identities persist."

block.name: Casual Slang and Filler

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero filler: "basically", "essentially", "literally" (non-literal), "I mean", "look", "listen", "so" (opener), "honestly", "actually" (uncorrecting).

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero fake enthusiasm: "wild", "insane", "mind-blowing", "incredible", "amazing", "fascinating" (unearned).

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero forced familiarity: "you guys", "folks", "friends", "we" (when no we), "right?" (mid-sentence), "let's be real", "here's the thing", "spoiler alert".

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero internet-speak: "hot take", "fun fact", "pro tip", "next-level", "deep dive", "low-key", "high-key", "vibe", "energy" (metaphorical).

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero inflated informality: "a ton of", "a bunch of", "super" (intensifier), "kind of"/"sort of" (hedging), "pretty much".

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero em dashes anywhere.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Casual slang and filler to avoid

block.rule: Zero dramatic fragmentation ("Sixty. Thousand.", "One word. Design.").

block.name: Tone Pitfalls

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Tone pitfalls to avoid

block.rule: Zero corporate dryness. No passive constructions that strip personality.

rule.ref: guide: Section 2, Tone pitfalls to avoid

block.rule: Zero salesman pressure. No urgency, scarcity, or fear.

block.name: Self-Check (judgment checks)
block.rule: Substitution test: no sentence works when topic is swapped for a different subject.
Aloud test: every sentence sounds natural in a calm adult conversation.

rule.ref: guide: Section 7, Who wrote this test

block.rule: Who-wrote-this test: a reader can tell a human wrote the draft.
So-what test: every statement is answered by the next sentence.
Deletion test: no sentence can be removed without the paragraph losing something.

block.name: Factual Accuracy
block.rule: Every factual claim presented as fact is true.
Invented stories never read as documented real cases. Zero fake names, dates, or institutions.
Every specific date, number, name, quote, or study is textbook-canonical, hedged, or omitted.

group.name: High-risk categories (100% verifiable or hedged/removed)
group.rule: Exact dates.
Specific statistics ("73% of people...").
Study citations (author, year, institution).
Direct quotes attributed to real people.
Obscure names (minor historical figures).
Superlatives ("the first", "the oldest", "the largest").
Book or paper titles.

block.rule: Package defaults to mechanism, not measurement.

group.name: Self-verification pass
group.rule: Every specific claim walked through individually.
Every claim clears 95% confidence.
Claims that did not clear are hedged, removed, or replaced with the mechanism.
Zero swapping one specific for another without verifying both.
High-risk categories re-checked after writing.

rule.ref: guide: Section 6, Do not fabricate facts to fill quotas

block.rule: Zero fabricated claims to fill a quota.

group.name: @course
group.rule: @course is the first line. Blank line, then metadata fields.
All 7 fields present: id, title, author, description, color, genres, relevant_for.
description is a single line.
color is a valid hex code.
id follows [type]:[kebab-case-title]-[4-char-hash].

group.name: @segment
group.rule: Every @segment has exactly 2 body lines: title, then 1-to-3 character uppercase symbol.
Every @segment is followed by at least one @package.

group.name: @package
group.rule: Every @package has at least one content block.
Title and optional "Free" flag come before any content declarations.
First package of first segment has "Free" on its own line.

rule.ref: RlockieFormatGuide.xml, Section 8, Mandatory package structure comment

group.rule: Every @package starts with a mandatory structure-comment line listing the swipe-type sequence of the package in order. Format: "# @text, @text, @text, @question, @text, @true_false, @reflect, @text". The comment goes immediately after the package title (and the "Free" line if present), and before the first content block. The comment list length equals the swipe count of the package. If the package uses an intro variant, the comment reflects it.

group.name: Package titles
group.rule: Title length is 2 to 7 words. One-word titles allowed occasionally, eight-plus-word titles forbidden.
Titles vary in shape across the segment. At most ONE "The X That Y" title per 5-package segment.
No two consecutive packages share the same syntactic shape (different shape, not just different nouns).

Title shapes used across the segment span multiple categories: question, open-ended fragment with three dots, short noun phrase, why/when statement, narrative beat, single concept fragment. Zero segments where every title is built from the same template.
Titles hint at the topic but never name it.
Zero subtitles, zero colons in titles ("Coffee Cups: A Study in Affordances" is wrong).
Zero banned words in titles ("things", "stuff", banned verbs, banned adjectives stay out of titles too).

Numbers in titles use digits, not words. This applies to segment AND package titles and overrides the "small numbers can stay as words" rule from the number-format section. "3 Layers of Feeling", not "Three Layers of Feeling".
Read-the-menu test: read the segment titles top to bottom. If they look like a list of variants of the same shape, the segment fails.

group.name: Comments
group.rule: Comment lines (any line whose first non-whitespace character is "#") are stripped by the parser before any other parsing. Comments never appear in the output. Comment content does not count toward any length rule, sentence count, or swipe count.
Inline comments are NOT supported. Only full-line comments (line starts with "#" after optional whitespace).

rule.ref: RlockieFormatGuide.xml, Section 8, Mandatory package structure comment, Line 1

group.rule: Every @package has the swipe-sequence comment as its first non-blank line after the title and Free flag. Format: "# @type, @type, ..." listing every swipe in order.

rule.ref: RlockieFormatGuide.xml, Section 8, Mandatory package structure comment, Line 2

group.rule: Every @package has the package summary comment directly under the swipe-sequence comment. Format: "# Variant X (N stories), [default|quote|true/false|misconception|skip] intro, teaching: [concept]". All three fields present, in this order, comma-separated.

rule.ref: RlockieFormatGuide.xml, Section 8, Mandatory package structure comment, Line 2

group.rule: The variant letter (A through G) in line 2 matches the Phase 2 variant actually used. The story count matches the number of @text swipes in Phase 2. The intro type matches line 1. The concept is the term that carries the green <c:g> highlight in Phase 4.

group.name: @text
group.rule: At least one line of text per @text block.
Color markup only per Phase 4 rules (green = topic, red = wrong, only in @text, max 1-2 per package).
Any image-link: is a broad categorical Wikimedia-style description. Surrounding text refers to the image.

group.name: @question
group.rule: Question text is exactly one line.
2 or 3 answer options, each followed by consequence:.
Blank line between each answer+consequence pair.
correct_answers present, 1-indexed.
explanation: present.
hint: present.

group.name: @true_false
group.rule: Statement is exactly one line.
Exactly one true: and one false: line, in that order.
The two answer options are always literally "True" and "False". The text after true: and false: is the consequence shown after the reader picks that option, not the option label.
correct_answers (1 or 2), explanation, hint all present.

group.name: @estimate
group.rule: Question is exactly one line.
answer: is an integer between 0 and 100.
explanation and hint are present.

group.name: @quote
group.rule: First line is the quote, no quotation marks.
Second line is the author, or empty.

group.name: @pause
group.rule: Exactly one line, one sentence.
No blank lines inside the body.

group.name: @reflect
group.rule: 2 or 3 thinking points, one per line.
Each point 14 to 16 words with a concrete example.

group.name: General formatting
group.rule: Zero trailing whitespace.
Zero tabs. Spaces only.
One blank line between last content block and next @package or @segment.
Properties (answer:, consequence:, correct_answers:, explanation:, hint:, true:, false:) use key-value syntax, not declarations.

result.pass: A draft passes only if every rule above holds.
result.fail: One failed rule means the draft goes back for a fix. The guide section named in the ref attribute is the only place that explains the rule and shows a compliant version.
